Each turn, players follow the declared suit (truthfully or not) and can challenge suspicious claims by calling Doubt. Correct Doubts steal points; wrong Doubts lose them. If no Doubt is called, the highest declared number wins the trick; if a Doubt occurs, the second-lowest number wins instead. With hidden cards, strategic lies, and custom wooden tokens for declarations, Angelion is a tense battle of bluffing, timing, and knowing when to trust — or betray.

Starguards of Orion is a solo role-playing wargame (RPG) where you take on the role of a Commander in the elite Orion Starguard — a law enforcement force operating in the fractured systems of the Orion Sector.

Like traditional RPGs, your Commander is your avatar. You will make strategic decisions about:

     Training and certifications
     Outfitting your Modular Command Ship (MCS)
     Leading tactical missions with your flotilla


Each mission challenges you to protect civilians, neutralize threats, and enforce order in a lawless frontier.

Your tactics are entirely your own but your greatest weapon is the Modular Command Ship (MCS), a vessel far superior to the obsolete Imperial raiders and repurposed civilian vessels you will face.

While enemy ships may swarm in numbers, your MCS combines advanced shielding, powerful weapon systems, and adaptive technologies that scale with your Commander’s skill.

Whether you overwhelm enemies with precision strikes, hold the line with reinforced armor, or disable fleets through electronic warfare, your ship is the centerpiece of each battle and a symbol of your growing influence.

Successful missions earn Influence and Honor, unlocking new systems, elite certifications, and stronger ships. As your reputation grows, so does your authority; leading to high-stakes confrontations with criminal cartels and hostile factions across the Orion Sector. 

—description from the designer

SGO Logo designed by Robert Lausevic (@thatrobert on BGG)

In this fast-paced, coffee-themed card game, 2-6 players can “order” or “take a sip” – playing any special cards picked up along the way – until they think they’ve collected the most points and “stop ordering”. But beware, not all beans are bountiful – draw the Hyper Crash card and it’s game over!

—description from the publisher

Greedy henchmen try to pocket stolen goods before the boss returns. Working together — but not too loyally — you'll snatch up gold, silver, and diamonds, hiding your loot under your hat at just the right moment. Push your luck too far, and you might get caught empty-handed when the boss shows up.

Snitch Trick is a trick-taking game where players must not follow suit, winning tricks to collect valuable cards while timing when to secretly stash them. Cards hidden under the hat score points, while unhidden cards can become costly penalties. The tension between hoarding more and hiding safely creates a chicken race of greed and caution, with each trick pushing players closer to a risky decision point.

Forming the world's greatest rock band takes more than talent... it takes cash, cunning, and perfect timing. As a shrewd producer, you'll chase star musicians like legendary guitarists, charismatic vocalists, genius drummers, and powerhouse keyboardists. But without the right parts to complete your songs, even the best lineup can't save you. Money talks, and in the ruthless music industry, it's survival of the richest.

■ Contents

Artist Cards
-Vocal (Vo): 2, 4, 6
-Guitar (Gt): 1, 2, 4, 5, 6
-Bass (Ba): 1, 2, 4, 5, 6
-Drums (Dr): 2, 4, 5, 6
-Keyboard (Ky): 2, 4, 6
-Vocal (Vo) & Guitar (Gt): 3
-Vocal (Vo) & Bass (Ba): 3
-Vocal (Vo) & Drums (Dr): 3
-Vocal (Vo) & Keyboard (Ky): 3

Note: The number on the card indicates the artist's ability as Artist Points (Pt).

Song Cards
-Lv. 1: 4 cards 
-Lv. 2: 4 cards 
-Lv. 3: 4 cards

Note: These cards show the artist parts needed to complete the song and the song's own points (Pt) when completed.

Scout Money (Suits: Red, Yellow)
-$200: 2 cards x Red & Yellow 
-$300: 3 cards x Red & Yellow 
-$400: 3 cards x Red & Yellow 
-$500: 2 cards x Red & Yellow 
-$600: 1 card x Red & Yellow 
-$700: 1 card x Red & Yellow

Raise Money
Money cards used in the Raise Phase. 
$100: 40 cards

Debt Slips
Paper for recording debt. 
1 pad (100 sheets)

■ Game Setup (For 4 Players)

Decide the lead player who will play the first card. The person who most recently attended a rock band concert is the lead player. If multiple people went on the same day, the person who had the seat closest to the stage is the lead player. If still undecided, use rock-paper-scissors or a similar method.

● Scout Phase Setup (Scout Money)

Take all Red and Yellow Scout Money cards ($200 to $700, 24 cards total), shuffle them face down to form a draw pile. Starting with the lead player and going clockwise, each player draws 2 cards from the draw pile into their hand. Again, starting with the lead player and going clockwise, each player draws 2 cards. They choose 1 to add to their hand and pass the other face down to the player on their left. Repeat this process once more. Each player will now have 6 Scout Money cards in their hand.

● Raise Phase Setup (Raise Money)

Gather all Raise Money cards ($100) and deal 10 to each player.
Keep Raise Money separate from Scout Money.

● Artist Card Setup

Shuffle all 24 Artist cards face down to create a draw pile and place it in the center, visible to all players. The lead player flips over the top 2 cards from the draw pile and places them face up in the play area.
These are the two artists available to be signed this round. The player who bids the highest amount gets first pick of these two, and the player with the second-highest bid signs the remaining artist.

● Song Card Setup

Shuffle the 4 Lv. 1 Song cards face down and deal one to each player. Players place this card face up in front of them. Keep the remaining Lv. 2 and Lv. 3 Song cards (4 of each) in separate piles by level. Players can look at the Song cards at any time to check the required artist parts.

Remaining Song Cards (Lv. 2 / Lv. 3)
If a player fulfills the artist part requirements on their current Song card, they can acquire a Song card of the next level (Lv. 2 or Lv. 3).

● Setting up a debt voucher

Prepare the Debt Slip pad. Players will use it to record their debts. When taking a debt, players will mark it on a slip.

Designer's Notes
Artist cards include parts for Vocal (Vo), Guitar (Gt), Bass (Ba), Drums (Dr), Keyboard (Ky), and also multi-role artists like Vo/Gt, Vo/Ba, Vo/Dr, Vo/Ky. The numbers 1-6 on the cards represent the artist's skill level. Your goal is to gather artists with high numbers!

● Scout Phas

For the 2 Artist cards revealed in the play area, players will play Scout Money as an initial bid. The purpose of this phase is to qualify for the Raise Phase and to gain a favorable turn order in it.

▼ Only Two Artists Can Be Signed!

The two players who have bid the highest total amounts across the Scout and Raise Phases can sign (acquire) an artist.

▼ Lead Player's Play

The lead player chooses one Scout Money card (either Red or Yellow) from their hand and plays it face up.
The suit of this card (Red or Yellow) becomes the lead suit for this round.

▼ Other Players' Play (Clockwise)

Starting from the player to the left of the lead player and going clockwise, each player also plays one Scout Money card from their hand.

▼ If You Have Scout Money of the Lead Suit

You must play one card of the same suit. By playing a card of the same suit as the lead player's Scout Money, you can proceed to the Raise Phase. The amount played can be any value.

▼ If You Do Not Have Scout Money of the Lead Suit Choose one of the following actions:

A) Play Scout Money of a Different Suit ⇒ Cannot participate in the Raise Phase Play one Scout Money card that is not of the lead suit. This player cannot participate in this round's Raise Phase.

B) Substitute with Raise Money ⇒ Can participate in the Raise Phase If you don't have Scout Money of the lead suit, you can pay $200 from your Raise Money (two $100 bills) to substitute for it. You cannot do this if you do not have two $100 bills. (Paid Raise Money is sent to the discard pile even if you don't sign an artist.)

Example of Scout Phase
The available artists are a Vocal/Keyboard (3Pt) and a Guitar (5Pt). Player A wants Vo, Gt, and Vo parts, so they bid Red: $500! Player B only needs Vo and Gt, so they aim for 2nd place with Red: $300. Player C doesn't have a Red suit card, so they play a Yellow suit card and drop out of the bidding for artists this round. Player D also doesn't have the lead suit but wants Vo, Gt, and Vo parts, so they pay Raise Money to participate in the Raise Phase, hoping to win a contract!

Designer's Notes
Playing a high-value Scout Money card in the Scout Phase gives you a significant advantage: you get to act last in the Raise Phase. This allows you to see other players' moves before bidding your Raise Money, giving you a tactical edge.
Even if you don't have the lead suit, you can substitute with Raise Money.
For artists you desperately want, it might be better to take on debt to acquire them…

● Raise Phase

Players who played the lead suit (or substituted with Raise Money) in the Scout Phase proceed to the Raise Phase to conduct final contract negotiations with the artists.

▼ Determining Turn Order

Turn order proceeds clockwise, starting with the player who bid the lowest value on their Scout Money card in the Scout Phase. The player who played the highest value Scout Money card will go last. If multiple players bid the same amount, the player closest to the lead player (in clockwise order from the lead player) goes first among them.

▼ Actions During Your Turn

When it's your turn, perform one of the following actions (① or ②):
"Debt" can be taken any number of times on your turn, regardless of choosing ① or ② (up to a game maximum of 5 times).

① Raise!

Add Raise Money to the Scout Money you've already bid. You can add any amount of Raise Money you possess. It's possible to take a debt to pay for this.

Note: You don't have to raise to the highest bid. Since you can acquire an artist even in 2nd place, bidding an amount aiming for 2nd place is a valid strategy! In case of a tie for bid amount, the player closer to the lead player is considered higher ranked (for 1st or 2nd place).

Note: Debt When you take a debt, put a checkmark by your name on a Debt Slip and receive $500 in Raise Money (five $100 bills) from the supply (if $500 is available in the supply!). Each debt taken results in -6 points. You can take a debt multiple times during your turn in the Scout & Raise phase, but only up to a maximum of 5 times per game.

② Do Not Raise! (Pass)

If you choose not to raise (pass), you cannot raise again for the remainder of this round.

※ Debt

When you place a check mark on the name of a Debt Slip, you receive Raise Money of $500 (5 × $100 bills) from the supply (discard pile)—if the supply has $500 available!
Each instance of taking debt results in “-6 points.”
You may take debt as many times as you like during your own Scout & Raise Phase, but only up to a maximum of 5 times in total.

End of the Raise Phase

The Raise Phase continues until all participating players choose "② Do Not Raise!" (pass). Once all players have finished raising (or passed), the Raise Phase ends.

At that point, the player with the highest total bid (Scout Money + Raise Money) is 1st, and the player with the second-highest total bid is 2nd. They proceed to sign (acquire) artists.

Designer's Notes 
When raising, consider which of the two available artists you need. The two players with the highest total bids (Scout Money + Raise Money) can sign artists. It's perfectly fine to deliberately bid an amount aiming for 2nd place! A smart contracting strategy involves watching other players' finances while making your raises. Try to win contracts for the lowest possible amount!

● Artist Card Contract

Once the Raise Phase ends, players sign artists based on their rank.

▼ Signing (Acquiring) Artists

The player who bid the highest total amount (1st place) chooses one of the revealed Artist cards and signs (acquires) it.

The player who bid the second-highest total amount (2nd place) signs (acquires) the remaining Artist card.

Note: Place acquired Artist cards face down in front of you.

Note: If only one player proceeded from the Scout Phase to the Raise Phase, that player signs (acquires) one artist, and the other is sent to the discard pile.

Note: If there are multiple players tied for the highest bid (1st place), or for the second-highest bid (2nd place), the player closest to the current round's lead player (in clockwise order) within each tied rank wins the tie and signs (acquires) the artist.

◯ Money Processing for Players Who Signed (Acquired) an Artist
Players who signed an artist send their total bid (played Scout Money and any added Raise Money) to the discard pile.

Note: Raise Money paid as a substitute for the lead suit in the Scout Phase is also sent to the discard pile.

× Money Processing for Players Who Did Not Sign an Artist
Players who did not sign an artist send their played Scout Money and any Raise Money paid as a substitute for the lead suit (in the Scout Phase) to the discard pile.
However, for Raise Money bid during the Raise Phase, only $100 is sent to the discard pile, and the rest of that Raise Money is returned to their hand.

Acquiring New Song Cards

Players who signed an artist check if they have now gathered all the required artist parts for their current Song card.

If all artist parts for the Song card are met, they can acquire a new Song card. (e.g., Lv. 1 complete → take Lv. 2; Lv. 2 complete → take Lv. 3). Choose one new Song card of the appropriate level and place it face up in front of you.

If, upon acquiring a new Song card, you immediately meet its requirements as well, you may consecutively choose and acquire the next level Song card.

Only your highest-level completed Song card will be scored at the end of the game. If you take a higher-level Song card but fail to complete it by game end, you will score for the previously completed lower-level Song card instead.

Simultaneous Completion: If multiple players complete their Song cards in the same round, the player closest to the current round's lead player (in clockwise order) gets to choose their new Song card first.

Designer's Notes 
Think carefully about which artist you want, the status of your own Song cards, and how much Scout & Deal Money you have, then decide how far you're willing to go in the bidding!
If you manage to sign a high-point artist, you might earn enough points to more than make up for the debt penalty.
On the other hand, you could also drive up the price on purpose and then pass, forcing other players to sign them at a high cost…?

Determining the Next Lead Player
The player who acquired an artist with the highest bid (1st place) becomes the lead player for the next round. If there was no 1st place player (e.g., only one bidder, or no successful bids), the previous lead player continues as lead player.

Redistribute Scout Money 
At the end of the 6th round, redistribute Scout Money. Collect all Scout Money from the discard pile and any remaining in players' hands. Shuffle them all together and redistribute them to each player following the same procedure as in the initial game setup (each player gets 6 cards).

(Normally, Scout Money should be mostly used up by the 6th round.) Raise Money is not redistributed; players keep their current Raise Money.

Preparing for the Next Round
Flip over 2 new cards from the Artist card draw pile and reveal them to the play area. Start the Scout Phase with the new lead player.

■ End of Game and Scoring

The game ends when all Artist cards have been revealed and the final (12th) round is completed. Proceed to score calculation.

▼ Scoring for Completed Songs 
Each player checks their highest-level completed Song card. Only the highest-level Song card for which all required artist parts have been gathered is used for scoring. Uncompleted Song cards are discarded and score no points.

Score for a completed song = [Song Points (Pt) printed on the Song card] + [Total Artist Points (Pt) of all Artist cards participating in that song].
(Song cards with even one missing required part score no points.)

▼ Example: A player has a completed song. 
The participating artists are Vo/Ky (3Pt) + Gt (5Pt) + Ba (5Pt) for a total of 13 Artist Pt. Their Lv. 1 Song requirements are met, granting them, for instance, 6 Song Pt. Their Lv. 2 song is not completed.

▼ Completing Multiple Songs 
If multiple Song cards are completed, only the highest-level one is scored. Example: If both Lv. 1 and Lv. 2 songs are completed, you score for the Lv. 2 song.

■ Scoring Remaining Hand Money

Raise Money remaining in hand is converted to Pt. For every $200 of Raise Money, 1 Pt is added. Any remaining $100 is rounded down (worth 0 Pt).
(Scout Money remaining in hand does not score points.)

Example: If you have 5 Raise Money cards ($500) left, $200 = 1 Pt, so you get 2 Pt (from $400). The remaining one $100 bill is not worth any Pt.

Debt Deduction Check your Debt Slip and count the number of checkmarks you've made. Each debt checkmark is a deduction of -6 Pt. Example: If there are 3 checkmarks on your Debt Slip, 3 x -6 Pt = -18 Pt.

Final Score Calculation Calculate the final Pt score using the following formula: Final Score (Pt) = [Total Pt from your highest Completed Song] + [Remaining Hand Money Pt] - [Debt Pt]

The player with the highest final Pt score is the winner.
If multiple players are tied for the highest Pt, the player among them who signed the most Artist cards during the game is the winner. If still tied, all tied players are winners!

The winning player is lauded as a true shrewd producer who assembled the best band members and put on the greatest live show!

■ 3-Player Setup

When playing with 3 players, make the following adjustments to card setup.
Other than the adjustments below, the rules and procedures are the same as for a 4-player game.

Scout Money Adjustment
Before starting the game, remove the following Scout Money cards from the game:

-Red Suit $200: 1 card 
-Red Suit $300: 1 card 
-Red Suit $400: 1 card 
-Yellow Suit $200: 1 card 
-Yellow Suit $300: 1 card 
-Yellow Suit $400: 1 card

Raise Money Adjustment
Deal 13 Raise Money cards ($100 each) to each player. Place the one leftover $100 card in the discard pile.

The remaining Scout Money (18 cards) is dealt so that each player gets 6, following the same procedure as in a 4-player game.
The setup for Artist cards, Song cards, and Debt Slips is the same as for a 4-player game.

boardgame: 仲良シふれんず りにゅーある (Nakayoshi Friends Renewal)
description:
The friends are back, bringing even more companions along for chaotic adventures! In this lively world, the goal isn’t to dominate, but to dodge — players must cleverly avoid winning tricks while navigating new rules introduced by quirky new characters. Growing friendships come with growing headaches, where managing the crowd becomes its own challenge.

Nakayoshi Friends Renewal is a trick-taking game where players must follow suit but avoid winning tricks, with no trump suit and new combo rules. Winning tricks causes players to lose points, while special cards that are multiples of three allow chaining lower-numbered cards of different colors, opening up strategic escapes and sudden pile-ups. Balancing when to follow, dodge, or combo defines the path to survival in this colorful showdown.

Joining the ranks of the Magic Corps MAGi MiLES, players train in elemental mastery through intense magical duels. Mastery demands not just raw power but clever manipulation of the four elemental attributes, guiding your chosen magic to victory and proving yourself the most skilled commander in the corps.

MAGi MiLES is a trick-taking game where players must follow suit, using the hidden backs of cards to navigate elemental battles across two plays per trick. Careful planning and sharp reading of opponents are crucial to mastering the magical battlefield.

boardgame: 十二支乱世-エトランゼ (Juunishi Ransei: Etranze)
description:
Divine power reshapes the zodiac in a battle of wits and will. As new gods, players propose a fresh roster of zodiac animals, using supernatural abilities to guide selections and create a unique lineup beyond tradition. Clever manipulation and bold choices decide who earns the right to define the next great 十二支 (twelve signs).

Juunishi Ransei - Etranze is a trick-taking game where players compete over animal cards in a selection phase before assembling sets for final scoring. Across 12 rounds of trick-taking with variable suits and trump rules, losing a trick earns your played card while winning lets you claim a public reward. Players can also activate divine powers twice per game at the cost of discarding cards, trading hand strength for tactical advantage. After the selection phase, collected cards are grouped into sets to form new zodiacs, with complex combinations awarding higher points. The player with the most 奇想点 (victory points) declares their zodiac supreme.
publishers: 限界サムズアップ (Maximum Thumbs Up)

boardgame: トリックハンティング (Trick Hunting)
description:
In a lively forest teeming with wildlife, bumbling hunters and clever animals engage in a lighthearted battle of wits. Armed with lassos, nets, slingshots, and even timid hunting dogs, hunters try to capture their elusive prey — while sleepy deer, cautious foxes, and feisty bears fight back in a charmingly clumsy chase through the woods.

Trick Hunting is a trick-taking game where players must follow suit, using cards with dual hunter and animal sides to navigate a whimsical predator-prey battle. Tricks revolve around capturing animals with hunters or evading hunters with animals to score points. Players alternate strategies based on the role each card plays, aiming to gather the most points before hands are exhausted.

Long ago, humanity lived without knowing the joy of drawing cards... until Galileo, inspired by the pull of gravity, supposedly cried, "Why not just draw?!" In the spirit of this fictional "Law of Universal Draw," players now dive into a new kind of trick-taking where every move reshapes their hand, blending clever setup and on-the-fly adaptation.

Tsumooo! is a trick-taking game where players must follow suit and draw a new card from special draw decks after every play. At the start, players create draw piles, sneaking cards into them as traps or opportunities. Choosing where to draw from and managing what others left behind becomes as important as playing the right cards. To win, players must fulfill their declared trick targets, adapting constantly as their hand evolves.

boardgame: The Magic Hand and Three Elements
description:
A battle of wits and magic unfolds where every player crafts their own hand from scratch. Managing magical energy between fire, water, and grass elements, players create their personal arsenal to outsmart, overpower, or outmaneuver their rivals. With perfectly fair starting conditions, only clever planning, psychological reading, and bold plays will crown the next magic king.

Mahō no Tefuda to Mittsu no Eremento is a trick-taking game where players build their own hands before battling under a three-element rock-paper-scissors system.
Each player assigns numbers and elements to seven cards, distributing a set amount of magical power. During the trick phase, cards clash through element superiority and value comparison, with victories scored directly from card numbers. Light rules, strategic depth, and customizable difficulty make every match a balance of mind games and mastery.
publishers: もんきちゲームズ (Monkichi Games)

description:
VHS editions include Desfasados expansion.


Two identical decks face off, nothing more.

Each player draws 10 cards from their deck. From then on, like in the post-apocalypse, it's all strategy and chance.

Each player has a deck of 33 cards. Both decks are equal.

Each player draws 10 cards at random from their deck and may change 1 or 2 before starting.

These cards are played for up to 3 rounds. The player who wins two rounds wins.

The playing area is a battlefield in which each player has two rows (a front row and a back row) facing each other.

Players decide (randomly or by agreement) who is the leader, that is, who starts the first round. The opposing player starts the second round. If there is a third round, the leader starts it.

Cards are played in turns. Each player uses their turn to play a card in one of their two rows, whichever suits them best.

Each round ends when both players say PASS. The player who says PASS first stops playing. Then, the other player can make as many plays as they want until they say PASS. When the second player says PASS, the round ends.

Each player's points are tallied, and the player with the most points wins the round. If there is a tie, both players win that round. If the tie occurs in the third round, the game ends in a tie.

The cards from the completed round go to each player's discard pile, and a new round begins with the cards remaining in each player's hand.

The expansion for two players. With a 3D effect!
Plugs into the original game to add a layer of complexity.

Includes 9 cards per player (18 total):

6 PHASE-OFF characters
2 DUO-PHASE-OFF effects
BONUS: 1 new card for the original deck: REPLACES RIVAL
INCLUDES 3D GLASSES (1) TO SEE THE PHASE-OFFS IN THEIR DIMENSION.

Recommended for advanced players of the original game.

boardgame: Arrows and Quarrels: Large Scale Medieval Skirmish Rules
description:
Arrows and Quarrels is a fast paced set of wargame rules aimed at the medieval period. 
The rules use a card and dice mechanism, aiming to bring the fun and suspense back into war gaming.

They are designed to allow large games with lots of commands and are suitable for multi-player games for an evening, or longer.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Will Victoria Be Amused? Rules for Colonial Wargaming in the Age of Victoria
description:
Will Victoria be Amused is a set of fun rules for the Sudan campaign based on a dice and card mechanism, aimed to bring the fun and suspense back into wargaming.

All players are Allied Officers who are attempting to deliver a ‘jolly good show’ and gain promotion at the expense of others, whilst always maintaining a stiff upper lip in the face of danger.

The Sudanese will be controlled by a strict regime of charge and kill the enemy. No rallying or fancy tactics are involved, just continuous attacks that keep going for the kill.

This regime will soon tell on the Allies, making their mission increasingly difficult and thereby ensuring the players will need to help their fellow officers. But just enough to avoid mission failure, as victory points will only come to those who put on a good show and personally seize the glory.

Can your officer be the recipient of the Colonial Medal or even a Victoria Cross?

It’s up to you!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: China and the World Dance: Skirmish Rules for Boxer Rebellion
description:
China and the World Dance is a set of rules for the Rebellion and uprising of the Righteous Fists of Harmony or Boxers in an alliance with the Empress Dowager Cixi and her Imperial Chinese forces against the Foreign powers and Christians who are trying to slice up China into manageable partition’s to allow the riches and trade to flow into their coffers. Allowing players to recreate the open warfare and firepower of the day in a way that emphasises the role played by the Gentlemen and Officers of the period. Players will need to seize control of every situation, in order to win the day with the latest technical innovations from some of the most powerful Empires in the free world.

The Boxers will be controlled by a strict regime of charge and kill the enemy. No rallying or fancy tactics are involved, just continuous attacks that keep going for the kill.

This regime will soon tell on the Allies, making their mission increasingly difficult and thereby ensuring the players will need to help their fellow Legations. But just enough to avoid mission failure, as victory points will only come to those who put on a good show and personally seize the glory.

The rules use a card and dice mechanism, aiming to bring the fun and suspense back into wargaming. They are designed to allow large games with lots of commands and are suitable for multi-player games for an evening, or longer.

China and the World Dance aims to give a feel for the period and the way command and control worked in the war, where loyalty to your religion and Empire were as much a part of battle as good training and armaments. They make no claim to be overly historically accurate.

Will you win through or will you be sent home in disgrace.

It’s up to you!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Carry on up the Raj: Wargame Rules for the Indian Uprising 1857
description:
A set of fun rules for the Indian Uprising based on a no-dice mechanism, aimed to bring the fun and suspense back into war gaming.

These rules attempt to allow players to recreate the open warfare and horror of the 1857 Indian Uprising in a way to emphasise the role played by the Gentlemen and officers of the period. They will need to take control of the circumstances to help win the day with dash and swagger.

They are designed to allow large games with lots of commands and are suitable for multi-player games for an evening or longer.

They make no claim to be historically accurate but give a feel for the period and the way command and control worked in the country where Hate and miss information drove the war onward in directions controlled by the British Empire, The East India company and the Indian Cast system and Religions.

Most of all they are designed to be fun and played in the manner of Gentlemen unlike the real war.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Zulu's Thousands of Them? Skirmish Game Rules
description:
Zulu’s Thousands of Them? is a set of fun rules for the Zulu campaign based on a dice and card mechanism, aimed to bring the fun and suspense back into wargaming.

All players are Allied Officers who are attempting to deliver a ‘jolly good show’ and gain promotion at the expense of others, whilst always maintaining a stiff upper lip in the face of danger.

The Zulu’s will be controlled by a strict regime of charge and kill the enemy. No rallying or fancy tactics are involved, just continuous attacks that keep going for the kill.

This regime will soon tell on the Allies, making their mission increasingly difficult and thereby ensuring the players will need to help their fellow officers. But just enough to avoid mission failure, as victory points will only come to those who put on a ‘good show’ and personally seize the glory.

Can your officer be the recipient of the Colonial Medal or even a Victoria Cross?

It’s up to you!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Zulu's Thousands of Them? Big Battle Rules
description:
Zulu’s Thousands of Them? is a set of fun rules for the Zulu campaign based on a dice and card mechanism, aimed to bring the fun and suspense back into wargaming.

All players are Allied Officers who are attempting to deliver a ‘jolly good show’ and gain promotion at the expense of others, whilst always maintaining a stiff upper lip in the face of danger.

The Zulu’s will be controlled by a strict regime of charge and kill the enemy. No rallying or fancy tactics are involved, just continuous attacks that keep going for the kill.

This regime will soon tell on the Allies, making their mission increasingly difficult and thereby ensuring the players will need to help their fellow officers. But just enough to avoid mission failure, as victory points will only come to those who put on a ‘good show’ and personally seize the glory.

Can your officer be the recipient of the Colonial Medal or even a Victoria Cross?

It’s up to you!

—description from the publisher

Canewdon is a trick taking card game for four to five players.  The card-play uses standard rules of trick-taking.

Canewdon differs from many trick-taking games because the players determine the value of various objectives during the course of the hand.  They do this by placing cubes on the bidding sheet after the second, fourth and sixth trick.  This gradual reveal of the value of each objective and what each player hopes to achieve presents opportunities to change plans as the hand progresses, boost the value of an insignificant-seeming objective, and avoid making an objective an opponent seems likelier to achieve more valuable.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Fry's Chocolate Selection Box Rocket Race
description:
Fry's Chocolate Selection Box Rocket Race was a promotional space game that was printed on the lid of a chocolate box.

The game is a simple roll and move of carboard rockets along a winding path, encountering certain squares that will aid or hinder progress.

Players launch their rockets from England, passing a space station and Martian saucers similar to the 1953 movie War of the World. First to reach the sun is the winners

—user summary

You are farmers, recruiting cows as allies to try eliminate enemy farmers. Scientists tried making cows with more desirable beef and heightened breeding effiency. Although their original experiment was a success, it came with a strange side effect, a thirst for weaponry.

Common Cows is a fast paced card game where the aim is to survive the longest.  Each player takes turns playing a card from their hand to either grown their own health or to damage others.

Watch out as you hand can be stuffed by the portable stuffing cards, which may force you to play cards you don't want to.

In Slice you are picky citrus sellers swapping citrus segment cards to create delectable displays of three citrus slice circles. You will manipulate the market, stash secret seeds, and pick the perfect pieces, to plant the juiciest fruits in your display over 6 market rounds.

—description from the designer

All NEW Flamecraft mini-expansions for the base game.


     15 Hydragons - Dual Hydragons are coming to town! Each dragon has a unique “fire up” ability. Mix in a team of 6 into your artisan dragon deck!
     1 “Parks & Crafts” Neoprene Mat - A new way to play in the park! Dragons can “fire up” once per turn. 
     1 NEW SHOP! - Double Dragons is a new starter shop in the game. Add this charming courtyard shop where dragons are “dualing”.
     18 Upscale Cards - A new way to enchant. The Elder statue is glittered with Upscales. When you Enchant you can gift goods and coins to gain magical rewards!
     6 Companion Dragons - More companion dragons have wandered in to town and are looking to help! 
     7 Adventurer Dragons - Six adventure artisans can be added to your dragon deck. One is too fancy for that. 


—description from the publisher

KAOS: The game where betrayal rules and only one will restore order

In a world once ruled by peace and cooperation, a dark power changed everything: KAOS was unleashed, and with it came the Master of Chaos. Now, amid rumors, sabotage, and broken alliances, each player must rely on deception, strategy, and stealth to fulfill their secret objective and restore balance… if they can trust anyone at all.

KAOS is a fast-paced card game where players must gather five specific professions to complete their Objective Card. But it won’t be easy—rivals can launch sabotages, defend their own towns, or trigger events that turn the game upside down. Only the one who manages to keep their town intact by the end of their turn, evading sabotage, will emerge victorious.

The key lies in subtlety, cunning, and knowing when to strike or defend. With cards like firefighters, mayors, zombies, rumors, and political upheavals, the battlefield is full of chaos and opportunity. Will you complete your mission before the others?

Get ready for a dynamic game full of surprises and betrayals hidden behind friendly smiles. Welcome to KAOS… are you ready to survive?

—description from the publisher
family: Organizations: Board Games Association of Creators in Spain (LUDO)

description:
Welcome, mortal. Step aboard my boat, and let me guide you across the River Styx.
Before you lies Daemonia, the capital of the Underworld, a city brimming with life in its own dark, twisted way. Here, you'll find black-market merchants, contract brokers, blood magic practitioners, and those lost to the haze of forbidden pleasures.

Due to our ruler’s unexpected disappearance, the city is in a state of disarray. The Stygian Council, the city’s ruling body, is searching for Lightbringer's successor. The Watchers, trying to succeed to the throne, are competing against one another for the Council’s favour by amassing an army of monsters, all while trying to stab each other in the back.

However, do not worry about politics. Let me tell you some things about the Hellscape. Beware the surrounding volcanoes; they are home to Cerberuses that hunt anything that dares to stray too close. But not all is grim—Phoenixes, with their fiery beauty, are a sight to behold, and worth the perilous journey.

If you venture beyond the city, keep your wits about you. Should you cross paths with a Malebranchi, run. These creatures will carry you off to their nests high in the mountains, where Harpies and Griffins await with a hunger for blood. Your only hope lies in encountering a Nephilim—rare, but friendly souls who wander the Fields of Asphodel, where safety can sometimes be found.

As you walk along the banks of the Styx, you may hear the haunting melody of a Siren Song. Do not approach—their craving for human flesh is insatiable, and the nearby lakes are home to monstrous Hydras, waiting for their next victim.

In Daemonia, there is one unbreakable rule: strength above all. Demonstrate your power, and the creatures of this land will kneel before you. All of them.

Even the legendary Demons that lurk in the Void—unique and formidable, not easily subdued, but if you can earn their respect, they will serve you.

So there you have it. Appease to Council's Demands, gather followers, beat the other contenders and seize the Daemonia's crown.
—-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Daemonia contains:
16 Watcher cards 
7 Demon cards
20 Styx Hellscape cards
20 Asphodel Hellscape cards
20 Hermon Hellscape cards
20 Avernus Hellscape cards
20 Acherusia Hellscape cards            
21 Contract cards
28 Emporium Item cards,
12 Infusion cards
10 CD cards
20 Bonus Item cards
12 Custom 6-sided dices
30 Soul Coins
Rulebook, + Almanac
Game board, 
8 Player figurines, 4 Player boards 4 Cheat sheets, 1 First player marker

—description from the designer

Get ready to hit the track, go full throttle, and outrace your opponents to the checkered flag. Race-Crib uses Cribbage based play and scoring to advance players’ cars around the track.  Players will need to make well timed lane changes, as well as managing fuel and tires, to avoid unwanted contact or breakdowns. Winning usually requires a favorable roll of the die and some good “Racing Luck”.  Happy racing!

Colegas del Barrio is a quick card game where players build a street of 7 houses, placing numbered cards in order.
The goal is to avoid placing the same animal or costume in adjacent houses.
It’s a fun, fast-paced game that boosts logic and visual perception, perfect for kids and families.

The players take turns to roll and move. On some spaces events hinder or benefit the players. The first to reach the center space 39 wins the game.

On the game sheet there are blank spaces, where the kids should place fitting rubber-stamp-pictures and color them.

—user summary

A finite, drawless abstract strategy game where players detach stones from their groups and relocate them to form new groups or strengthen existing ones. There are no captures. In some cases, stones may even swap positions. The game ends when no further moves are possible, and the last player to make a move wins.

—description from the designer

The players form two teams. The pieces are placed alternately on the outermost six spaces. Then, the die is rolled in turn. However, the piece may initially only advance on the outermost ring if the player rolls the number of its space (I to VI). If a piece is now on one of the alphabetically designated spaces, it advances along the line to the other space corresponding to the number rolled. To cross the water (blue ring), a 6 must be rolled, as indicated by the number. Anyone who crosses the water cannot return; they must add up the total of the rolled die until they reach or exceed 10; then they may enter the central space (fortress). The winner is the team whose players reach the central space first, i.e., storm the fortress.

If a player finds the thrown space already occupied by an opponent, they capture that opponent, meaning the opponent must start over. Double casting is only permitted by players from the same party. On the marked spaces events hinder or benefit the players.

—user summary

Buddy Bridge is a two-person, trick taking card game utilizing a standard deck of cards and both jokers played in two phases: The Exchange and The Auction.

In The Exchange phase of play the players are dealt 12 cards and a joker. The players use these cards to build their future hand by winning tricks for the remaining available undealt cards. In the Auction phase the players will analyze and use their built hand to bid on their desired trump suit (or none) and the number of tricks they predict they will take (make a contract.)

Players who fulfil or defend against a contract receive both game and match points.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Эволюция волшебных тварей: Эфирные создания (Evolution: Another World – Aethereal Beings)
description: Эволюция волшебных тварей: Эфирные создания ("Evolution: Another World – Aethereal Beings") mini-expansion adds 11 new Evolution cards that can be played only as traits. These are the Aethereal trait cards. Only creatures who have witnessed a transmutation can gain such a trait.
Flip cards, count the bugs, and be the first to slap any card with a number matching the total bug count. Stay alert! New cards mean new opportunities to slap and score.

boardgame: Эволюция волшебных тварей: Жертвы волшебства (Evolution: Another World – Price of Power)
description:
Эволюция волшебных тварей: Жертвы волшебства ("Evolution: Another World – Price of Power") mini-expansion adds 8 new traits with powerful effects, but your creatures will have to pay for resolving them by sacrificing other traits.

Includes 16 cards with 8 traits, two cards each.

boardgame: Эволюция волшебных тварей: Редкие виды (Evolution: Another World – Rare Species)
description:
Эволюция волшебных тварей: Редкие виды ("Evolution: Another World – Rare Species") expansion contains additional components for the 5 or 6-player game mode.

This module includes 32 Evolution cards with the same traits as in the base game, but different art. They can be introduced into the game in two ways:


     If you want to increase the variety of illustrations in any regular game, remove from the Evolution deck 32 cards that are exact copies of other cards. Shuffle in the 32 Rare Species cards instead.
     If you want to play with 5 or 6 players, add the Rare Species cards to the Evolution deck without removing any other cards. Also, use the additional components for the 5 or 6-player game mode.


For 5-6 players, the win condition also changed: a player who gains 2 Transmutation tokens first wins the game.

boardgame: Эволюция волшебных тварей: Фамильяр (Evolution: Another World – Gnasher)
description: Эволюция волшебных тварей: Фамильяр ("Evolution: Another World – Gnasher") is an automated opponent. You can add it to any game as an additional player. It acts according to a simple algorithm and does not require much table space. At first, Gnasher is harmless and almost tame, but if you do not pay attention to it, it can go berserk and win in one turn.

Moonrakers micro-expansion that was part of the Veiled Fate: Tribunal Expansion's Kickstarter.

Includes 3 new crew member cards: Arbiter, Steward, Hadria

Steward: If you have 1 less Prestige than another player, subtract 1 from any requirement. +1 Credit, +1 Action.

Arbiter: +1 card to each player with more Prestige than you; +1 Action to each player with less Prestige than you.

Hadria: Subtract 1 from any requirement for each player with more Prestige than you. +1 Card, +1 Action.

Welcome to Dirty Rotten Scalpers, your front row ticket to backstreet bargains, bribes, and busts. To win, you'll need to wheel, deal, and even steal when buying and selling tickets to 20 spectacular sold-out events. Sounds like a pretty simple hustle right? Not so fast. Around every corner lurks another ruthless scalper plotting to score a piece of your action. And sooner or later,  you'll meet a bunch of nosy cops who will stop at nothing - short of a delicious donut - to bust your hard-earned deal. In Dirty Rotten Scalpers, simple strategy and pure luck cleverly combine to deliver high-speed hilarity and family-friendly fun!

boardgame: Business Class: An Airline Management Game
description:
You’re an ambitious CEO – ready to set your company’s trajectory to ever greater heights. Can you lead your employees to build a successful airline from the ground up?

In this game, players take turns placing employees from their Headquarters on various locations where they execute actions to grow their airlines, or recalling all of their employees back to their Headquarters so that they can react to current events in the industry. Once the employee is placed at a location and completes its action, it stays there and can’t be used again until it has been returned to its player’s Headquarters (via bumping or recalling).  During actions, employees interact with cards and resources: played Hub cards grant landing rights at airport terminals, and Route cards are played on top of Hubs to schedule flights. Played Route cards generate resources to reinvest in the airline and also advance a figure on the Flight Tracker: when the figure reaches the last space, the game immediately ends.  The player with the most victory points wins.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Behemoth: A Solo Roll-&-Write Boss Fight Game
description:
The year is 3050. Explorers have begun to chart the furthest depths of the universe, but have discovered that these areas are inhabited by moon-sized monstrosities known as Behemoths... and each one is slowly moving toward Earth. A specially trained group of starship pilots called Hunters has been equipped and tasked with defeating the Behemoths before they become a threat.
You are a Hunter. Will you destroy the Behemoth before it destroys you?

Behemoth is a single player R&W game that aims to mix the boss fight style of classic Zelda games with the skill tree progression of RPGs, all overlaid with a futuristic science fiction theme.

—description from the designer

Bingo Adventure is a weird co-op adventure game that uses a Bingo set as the primary randomizer and action mechanic. During play you'll spot and spend specific patterns on your bingo card (or the team’s bingo card) to trigger special abilities and defeat the monster before the monster knocks out one or more of your teammates.

—description from the publisher

Inside the chaotic halls of Parliament, a fierce battle rages not for laws, but for popularity! Players act as party members fighting for public support, navigating backroom deals, opportunistic alliances, and wild betrayals. Clever use of animal-themed parties, sneaky bats, and persuasive speeches will determine who rises and who falls in this political free-for-all.

Kokkai Gekijou (Parliament Theater) is a trick-taking game where players must follow suit, using trump suits, special cards, and backloaded victories to secure points for their political faction. Each trick, players aim to collect cards matching their party while avoiding damaging leaks and managing opportunistic neutral forces. With must-follow rules, trumps, and a twist where later plays overpower earlier ones, timing, bluffing, and manipulation are key to seizing influence. The first player to exceed 15 points wins the political arena.

In this sharp and stylish card game, every card holds two colors and only one can lead to victory. Players engage in fast-paced tricks where the dominant color can change mid-round, shifting the balance in unpredictable ways.

Switch Colors is a trick-taking game where each card shows two different colors and numbers, and a switch mechanism determines which color counts. Players each play one card per trick, and the winner is the player with the highest number in the currently active color. Strategic play lies in anticipating color shifts and managing dual-purpose cards.
publishers: アナログマジカル玉子堂 (Analog Magical Tamagodo)

boardgame: うそつきジャッジ (Usotsuki Judge)
description:
Two cosmic gods want the same star — but since their powers are equal, they’ve agreed to settle the matter with a battle of lies and logic. In a duel of bluffs and bold claims, players compete for victory stars, pretending to win tricks they may not have earned — and punishing each other with “fake moons” when lies are exposed.

Usotsuki Judge is a two-player trick-taking and bluffing game where only one player knows the truth, and the other must decide whether to call it. Each round, both players play a card face-down, and the lead player announces who "won" the trick. The opponent may challenge that declaration or accept it. Cards are then revealed: if the claim was false and the lie is caught, the liar receives a penalty; if the bluff goes uncalled, the liar earns the trick. First to earn two victory stars wins — but collect two fake moons, and you lose instantly.

In the chaotic world of Mazoo's drink bar, you're not just mixing sodas, you're mixing trouble. Each round, players secretly blend drinks, trying to avoid a disgusting disaster. Maybe that brownish drink really is cola... or maybe someone slipped in coffee, melon soda, or worse. Read faces, trust no one, and sip with caution.

Gebodoba is a bluffing and deduction game where players take turns adding hidden drink cards to a communal cup, trying not to exceed a mystery threshold. If you think the mix totals exactly 20–22, shout “Gebo-Uma!” and drink it yourself. If you think it’s gone over, yell “Gebo-Mazu!” and force the last player to drink it instead. But beware! The numbers might be fake, and strange cards like Tabasco or tomato juice add chaos. With damage points and elimination, the last player standing wins.
publishers: 世紀末Games (End of the Century Games)

description:
In the infamous Mazoo's drink bar, patrons mix bizarre concoctions of soda, melon, and mystery flavors. But someone always ends up chugging the nastiest blend... and if you're not careful, that someone is you. Each round is a balance between bluffing, deduction, and strategic poisoning-by-ice. Will you drink, or make someone else suffer?

Gebodoba Bar is a May Follow trick-taking game where players mix drinks and compete to avoid downing the "Gebodrink," a punishingly bad beverage.

Each trick, players add one card to the shared mix (the start player adds two), and the player with the most ice usually drinks. But if the drink contains colors different from the starter's, it becomes a "Gebodrink," and the most ice among those mixed colors determines who suffers the penalty. Trick winners lose stamina but gain points and if your stamina hits zero, your score resets. The tension lies in reading others, managing suits, and choosing when to risk that devastating sip. The game blends May Follow freedom with real Must Follow pressure, hand management, and clever risk-taking over two fast-paced rounds.
publishers: 世紀末Games (End of the Century Games)

description:
In a chaotic contest of animals with strong opinions and no fixed rules, players race to empty their hands but the rules of play change as often as the cards themselves. When you're stuck and can't play, there's only one solution: change the rules! Whether you're guided by a boar’s brute logic, a crab’s sideways thinking, or a dung beetle’s questionable taste, this game embraces absurdity and spontaneity in equal measure.

A Boar, Crab, Dung Beatle is a fast-paced, rule-shifting shedding game where players take turns playing cards to the center pile, aiming to be the first to run out. But when a player can’t make a legal move, they dynamically rewrite the active rule for card strength or interaction. It’s a game of adaptation, chaos, and outsmarting both the cards and the ever-mutating system.

—description from the designer
designers: イワムラ タクヤ (Takuya Iwamura)
artists: イワムラ タクヤ (Takuya Iwamura)
publishers: キューハチドッグ (Que Hachi Dog)

boardgame: With Musket, Fife & Drum: 18th Century Warfare Rules
description:
17 pages of rules and 7 pages of Army Lists for American War of Independence and Seven Years War.

Rules designed for use with 25mm or 15mm figures and use 6-sided dice both normal and average.

24 page paperback booklet with additional folded quick reference card.
family: History: War of the Austrian Succession
History: War of the Spanish Succession

description:
Curate a cozy little library in your neighborhood. Meet your personal goals or capture the hearts of the whole community by working together with your neighbors.

This puzzely book collection game has a twist. Your books are always rotating out of your library.

Visitors will take your book tokens and reward you in hear points or new book tokens to add to your collection.

Gain points by having the types of books visitors are looking for. Either by genre or popularity. Build up powerful turns by decorating your little library and landscaping around it.

—description from the publisher

Rise of Reign is a family-friendly strategy board game for 4–6 players, set in a magical realm where the king has fallen—and the empire is up for grabs.

In this battle royale-style game, players lead their own kingdoms and face off by summoning powerful creatures, empowering them with enchantments, or weakening their opponents’ forces. Players must manage two key resources—soldiers and coins—to stay in the fight and seize control.

A key twist? No one is ever truly out of the game. When your kingdom is conquered, you keep playing under the rule of your conqueror, keeping the game dynamic and unpredictable.

Add in the mysterious Forbidden Cards, and everything can change in an instant. These powerful cards can be your kingdom’s greatest fortune—or its complete ruin.

Every session plays out differently with new alliances, betrayals, and turning points. With a mix of strategy, luck, and resource management, Rise of Reign is built for families, casual gamers, and anyone who loves a fantasy-styled board games.

This is the Rise of your Reign.

—description from the designer

QuizPoker is a party game that blends the thrill of poker with the challenge of a quiz. Players must use both their knowledge and their bluffing skills to outwit opponents in a battle of brains and bravado. The game is structured in rounds, each beginning with a theme that sets the stage for the upcoming quiz question.

Each round starts with a first betting phase, just like in poker, where players place their initial bets without knowing the question. Once the question is revealed, players have the chance to rethink their strategies in a second betting phase. This setup allows players to bluff or play conservatively based on how confident they feel — or want others to think they are.

Even players who don’t know the answer can still win by bluffing effectively or reading their opponents’ behavior, making the game accessible and exciting for all types of players. The game’s poker-like structure includes increasing blinds, which adds pressure and intensity as the rounds progress.

The ultimate goal is to eliminate all other players by strategically managing your chips, making smart bets, and outlasting your opponents. Whether you’re a trivia master or a poker face pro, QuizPoker rewards both knowledge and cunning in equal measure.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Glory is Fleeting: Wargames Rules and Army Lists for the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars
description:
Glory is Fleeting (Glory) is a set of rules for using miniatures to recreate on tabletop the Revolutionary War and Napoleonic battles of 1792 to 1815. It is a comprehensive set of rules suitable for pick-up casual games, to recreate larger army-level historical battles and for the rigour of competition or tournament play.
The rules are divided into sections, each corresponding to a sequential phase in the process of setting up and playing a game of Glory. Coloured page sidebars differentiate the sections for easy location during play. Each section is prefaced by a conceptual explanation which (while not forming part of the rules) gives insight into what is being represented.
Following the rules are sections covering lines of communications, the use of redoubts, playing large historical refights and playing a shorter Glory 600 game. This volume then sets out over 160 comprehensive army lists from which forces can be constructed. A glossary rounds out the book.

—description from the designer

POTUSOPOLY is a bold, satirical board game where players dive into the chaotic, power-hungry world of American politics. Inspired by the classic mechanics of Monopoly but twisted for today’s political circus, this game invites you to bribe senators, manipulate scandals, and build your own corrupt political empire — all while dodging Guantanamo Bay and outwitting your rivals.

Instead of money, players use Political Power (PP) to keep score, make bribes, and control key positions in government. Once you control both senators and the governor of a state, you can start bribing Congressional Seats to increase how much PP your opponents owe when they land on your turf. Want to go legit? You can flip your politicians to "honest" — for strategic gains or survival.

Each space on the board represents a real political figure, government agency, or media outlet, all reimagined with biting humor. You'll draw from scandal decks, face random public outcries, and roll your way through a minefield of political chaos — from hot mics and laptop leaks to campaign scandals and shady news deals.

The goal is simple: gain control, dominate the board, and become the most powerful player in Washington. In POTUSOPOLY, there’s only one rule — win by any means necessary.

—description from the publisher

A new order has arrived at the design studio. Are you ready for a brand-new aesthetic challenge? This time, in addition to competing with each other, you will also start learning to cooperate.

Tailor Master: New Challenge 4+ Expansion is the first expansion of the game Tailor Master: Starter Set 3+. It upgrades the difficulty based on the original version and is suitable for children aged 4 and above (as well as adults). The cloth in this expansion feature more circular and ring-shaped patterns, with cyan, magenta, and navy blue as the main color schemes, bringing a new visual experience.

This expansion includes a set of new objective cards, two sets of acrylic frames, and new cloths.
You can use this expansion to play standalone 1v1 game; the rules are almost identical to those of the base game (Starter Set 3+); 
By combining it with the base game, you can play the new 2V2 team mode;
By combining it with the base game and the upcoming New Challenge 5+ Expansion, you can play the upcoming 3V3 mode.
In the team mode, players work together to outmatch their opponents by matching the clothes/pants patterns simultaneously or in relay.

—description from the publisher

Mesopotamia is a thematic civilization-building board game set at the dawn of human history. You’ll step into the role of a civilization, choosing from the Babylonians, Elamites, Akkadians, or Sumerians and guide your people from modest beginnings to enduring greatness.

Each civilization has its own unique strengths and flavor, drawing from historical roots to shape how you play. As you explore the shifting landscape, you’ll uncover mysterious lands and spark dramatic events that present tough choices. Your decisions could bring civilizations together or tear alliances apart in the blink of an eye.

Thanks to a modular, dual-layer board and hidden event tokens, no two games feel the same. The civilizations play differently every time, ensuring that each session is full of surprises and strategic depth. As the game progresses, you’ll unlock more actions — Build, Research, Agriculture, Gather and Trade, and Warfare — and those actions become more powerful. Tension builds until the Hanging Gardens rise, signaling the end of an epic journey.

The player with the most Victory points wins the game.

—description from the publisher
family: Constructions: The Hanging Gardens of Babylon

description:
Artemis Rift is a strategic tableau-building game set in the aftermath of a shattered moon. Players command one of four unique factions competing for control.

Build your fleet, gather resources, and claim locations to expand your influence. Each faction offers distinct advantages: economic prosperity, military might, scientific discovery, or territorial expansion. Increase your faction's affinity to unlock powerful end-game abilities that can turn the tide of conflict.

Balance tactical ship combat with resource management while racing to control the destiny of the galaxy. Will you uncover the truth behind the moon's destruction or simply forge a new order from its remains?

—description from the publisher

If you are a fan of bidding and trick taking this fast playing card game (3-5 players) is for you! It features a fun skiing/snowboarding theme sure to be a hit with not just skiers and boarders but anyone. Designed by Jeff & Kristin Irving.

The trump suit and hand count changes each hand making bidding a real challenge, depending on the roll of the die. Roll "Hit The Slopes!" on the die and you can pick trump after looking at your hand but roll "Yard Sale" (ski/board slang for crashing and your gear flying everywhere) and you lose your deal!

There are two decks: 
The playing card deck which features four suits (green circle, blue square, black diamond and double black diamond) and each card features the name of a well known ski slope.
AND
The hand count deck which determines how many cards each player will be dealt and, on even numbered hands, any bonus points players receive for bidding correctly that hand!

—description from the publisher

A strategy board game for 2 players released in Greece by MIKA.

Each player takes 6 ships of the same color and places them along the squares of one of the two coasts so that they can see the opponent's ships on the board.

The ships are moved using dice and the ships are moved horizontally or vertically back and forth, but never diagonally. Ships cannot pass over other ships or islands and when a ship ends up in a square where another ship is in an adjacent point then they have to fight using the dice. The winner is the player who rolls the highest number on the dice and the loser withdraws his ship from the game.

The winner of the game is the player whose ship occupies one of the squares of the opponent's coastline.

In this unusual table of alliances, your real power lies not in your own cards but in what your neighbors do with them. Each player is part of two shifting alliances: one with their left neighbor, one with their right. But you don't control your partnerships directly
.. instead, your card values fuse with your neighbors' suits to form alliance plays. Working together, sabotaging quietly, and watching both sides become key to victory.

Cena is a trick-taking game with a Must Follow rule where players do not win tricks based on their own card but through alliance cards created from the rank of their left neighbor's card and the suit of their right neighbor's card. Each trick awards points based on the card collected, but some cards are poisonous and reduce the score if taken. At the end of the game, a player's final score is the sum of the points earned by their two adjacent alliances, rewarding both collaboration and strategic deflection.

In a smoky jazz club filled with feline musicians, improvisation is everything. Each round flows like a syncopated rhythm, where playing the perfect note at the right moment is more important than overpowering the melody. As hands shrink and risks rise, only the most composed players can build the harmony they need.

Quintet is a trick-taking game in which players try to collect cards that sum exactly to five, while avoiding cards worth negative points. The lead player must play two cards instead of one, causing hands to shrink unevenly over time. The game ends when any player runs out of cards, and players score based on collected sets and remaining cards.
publishers: メリーアンドリューワークス(merry-andrew-works)

description:
In a kingdom of master blacksmiths, the king has issued a challenge: forge the finest gear using rare metals, and claim whatever is left as your prize. But the more victories you aim for, the fewer resources you'll have to forge them. Outguess, outplay, and outlast your rivals at the royal forge.

Trick Smith is a trick-taking game with Must Follow and a rotating trump system, where players bid on how many tricks they expect to win by placing cards aside from their hand. These cards cannot be used during the round, making bold bids riskier. Win a trick and place a card atop a matching bid to score; fail, and suffer a penalty. The game plays over three rounds, and the player with the most coins from completed bids wins.

boardgame: Puppy Stars: Master, Look at Me!
description:
Enter the world of adorable puppy performers vying for their owner's attention in a dazzling talent contest. Every round is a two-act performance, where timing, presentation, and crowd-pleasing charm matter more than raw power. Will your puppy become the ultimate star?

Puppy Stars is a trick-taking game for 2 to 6 players where each trick involves two cards per player, played one at a time, face-up or face-down. The highest combined appeal score wins the trick and earns a Star. Some cards have high value but come with special play conditions, requiring players to plan their combos wisely. Played over three rounds, the game tightens as previously used cards are removed from the pool, adding a layer of prediction and resource management. The player with the most Stars after three rounds wi
Both players, one using the red line cards, the other using the black line cards, try to create enclosed areas with lines of their colour.

Build the best pyramids on your side of the Nile in Ancient Egypt!

A River in Egypt is a 2 or 3 player linear territory game, where players compete to build the best collection of pyramids using Looney Pyramids. Using gameplay similar to Schotten Totten, players build the most impressive sets of pyramids on their side of the river, choosing where to allocate their pyramids.

The game uses 3 trios in five colors, and can be played with Pyramid Arcade. Players will also need a bag or bowl to randomly draw the pieces from. Players hands are always limited to one large, one medium, and one small pyramid, and when they play a pyramid they must replace it with another pyramid of the same size. A player wins when they either have claimed three adjacent river segments, or 5 out of the 9 total river segments.

A River in Egypt was released as part of the 321 trilogy of pyramid games.

—description from the designer

An exclusively three player dice game using Looney Pyramids. Players take on the role of a suitor in a love triangle where each player wants to change their personality to be a match with their crush (player on their left), while spurning advances from their own suitor (player on the right).

Players roll dice to change their display of pyramids to achieve this.

Love Triangle is part of the 321 collection of pyramid games.

—description from the designer

Well, Shoot! is a trick-taking game for four players that turns traditional bidding of games like Oh Hell on its head: instead of receiving a hand and choosing a bid, you are given a bid and must draft a hand that suits it.

Players each roll a die to determine their target number of tricks for that hand, and then draft cards to craft the perfect hand to hit that bid. Players who come closest to their bid will score more points. Players who don't come anywhere close to their bid will end up saying, "Well, shoot!"

You are a Time Agent locked in a battle with the opposition to manipulate the timeline to match your desired outcome!

Tricky Timelines is a timeline trick-taking game for 2 players, where players play cards to 9 tricks in any order, which are only resolved once all cards have been played to. The player who is best able to manipulate their timeline to match their bid wins the round.

Tricky Timelines can be played with a standard deck of cards, and was published as part of the Games with Cards booklet.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Lola High up in the Ocean
description:
Lola High up in the Ocean is a short solitaire micro game.

The young circus artiste Lola has crashed and is drifting in the ocean of her coma. Help her find the anchors to reality before she is trapped down there forever.

The cards line up to form dream sequences. Each time you add a new card, you decide whether to flip it over or not and whether to align or overlap it. Place a vitality token on the card's track and trigger the effect. If you run out of tokens, Lola is lost.
With each additional card, the previous tokens move further along the track and trigger effects. Try not to lose too many tokens along the way and keep an eye on Lola's heartbeat.
To lead Lola back to the world of the living, you must find and lift all three anchor cards.

—description from the designer

Team up and clean up!
Coop mode and new techs for shared strategy and combo plays.

—description from the publisher

Catch 33 is a dice based, flicking dexterity game using a ramp and two dice. It's typically 2 player head-to-head but can be played in teams.

Slide the first of your dice up the ramp to try and make it land in the cup. If you get the first shot in the cup, try to get the second one in, to add to your score. If both make it in the cup, add them together for your score and also set out a "defender post" on one of the four dots on the playfield. If your opponent knocks your post over on their turn, you get three points and get to reset it. If you accidently knock your own post over, it simply comes off the board. Getting four posts on the court is an instant win.

If your first die lands in the cup, and your second die lands outside the cup, but they land on the same numbers (two 1's,2s or 3s), you can chance it. Take the die outside the cup and shoot it again for more points... beware though, if you miss the "chance shot", then you score 0 points, thereby losing the points in the cup.

Scoring with double 1's ( snake eyes) scores 2 points, but also limits your opponent to one shot on their next turn (so the opponent's scoring opportunity is limited to one die, only if it lands in the cup, and no chance for a defender post).
family: Components: Dice Cup / Dice Tower / Dice Tray

description:
LISTEN, GUESS & BOOGIE

EUROVISION SONGS TIMELINE CARD GAME

Revisit all your favorite Eurovision songs from the last 69 years: from ABBA, to Loreen and Nemo. Best-of-the-best hits await you.

Gather your friends and get ready for an unforgettable game night with Eurovision songs timeline card game!

—description from the publisher

More raccoons, more combos!
New ability mixes using familiar mechanics for added variety every game.

—description from the publisher

Sneaky raccoon bosses add wild twists and new mechanics to the mix!

—description from the publisher

Each player receives two boss cards at the beginning of the game, giving them unique powers to use.

boardgame: Godtear: Durthax, Branchlord of the Weald & Treekin
description:
Unleash the unyielding wrath of nature with Durthax, Branchlord of the Weald.

Something extraordinary happened when a falling godtear pierced the bark of an ancient tree: its branches shuddered with life, and its trunk heaved roots from the clutches of the earth.  Durthax — protector and warden of the forest — was born. Along with their steadfast Treekin followers, Durthax aims to guard and nurture all living beings from those who seek to exploit them. A stalwart protector of all living things, Durthax embodies the brutal strength of nature, using their abilities to protect their four Treekin followers.

The Branchlord of the Weald is a hard counter to some of the toughest current champions in the game, with an unique range of stats and abilities. Slow and methodical, Durthax is unable to move during the plot phase, but makes up for it with a whopping 14 health, the highest of any Godtear champion. Durthax excels manipulating the field to your advantage, armed with the ability to teleport banners. They also work well with larger groups of small followers, helping defend them from danger with a strong set of support abilities.

In this box, you’ll find everything you need to plant Durthax in the thick of it, and start manipulating the battlefield to your advantage. Combine Durthax with either Godtear starter set, and you’ll have a warband ready to play.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Operation CanCon: Canadian Classic Music Shopping Gamebook
description:
Drop the Needle on One Last National Flashback.

Game Time, Canada!

It all comes down to this: the final verse in Operation CanCon. Leap between two capitals, across two centuries, on a mission to recover dozens of Canadian Content classics.

All you need is something to write with and three six-sided dice to roll your way through eight record stores in search of national treasures.

Equal parts branching-path adventure and bin-diving minigame, this interactive journey is a nostalgic tour of Canadian music. Whether you play for the memories or simply to know the score, this gamebook is your ticket to the past and, hopefully, a future.

—description from the designer

For information on how to play, see the Operation CanCon: Eighties video section at https://boardgamegeek.com/video/557062/operation-cancon-canadian-eighties-music-shopping/operation-cancon-how-to-play

Add even more raccoons for wild new twists every game.

—description from the publisher

New goal cards to bring fresh challenges for the lone scavenger.

—description from the publisher

Kryptothera is a competitive adventure game set in an alternate reality where cryptids — creatures from folklore — are found, and their energy fuels a radical new form of technology. Rival hunters journey across treacherous terrain in pursuit of these elusive beings, navigating a world shaped by myth, invention, and escalating ambition. The setting is infused with dark mystery and an art deco-inspired aesthetic, where technological marvels pulse beneath a veil of secrecy and superstition.

Kryptothera offers a competitive PvE experience. Each player takes on the role of a hunter with unique skill competencies that influence dice-based outcomes during cryptid hunts and challenge events. Players navigate a mapped region filled with unfolding events, acquiring items and hiring crew to improve their chances of success. Successfully capturing a cryptid is only part of the challenge — hunters must also return it to their headquarters to secure it. In the later stage of the game, players can initiate duels — introducing a PvP element where hunters may attempt to steal cryptids from one another, adding a layer of high-stakes rivalry before the hunt concludes.

The aim is to secure the most valuable collection of cryptids before the hunt ends. Players must balance risk and timing — deciding when to explore further, when to return to base, and when to confront their rivals. Every action shapes the outcome, and no lead is ever safe. Kryptothera rewards strategic planning, adaptability, and a sharp instinct for opportunity in a world where power shifts with every roll of the dice.

—description from the publisher

A party game where all players take turns in reading out really awkward scenarios, then choosing from three dodgy outcomes! The challenge then is for players to guess which dodgy decision was chosen?!

For example...

Well... this is really Awkward!

I'm on the flight from hell! I have a baby screaming beside me, a kid behind me who is constantly kicking me in the back, and the dude in front is on full recline pinning my tray table against me.

I'm completely surrounded, the flight is full, and I have the mother of all hangovers. If this doesn't stop soon, someone is going to die! I can't take it anymore, so I...


     Notice a spare seat in business class and slip the flight attendant a lazy $200 and ask to move there
     Head to the toilet and lock the door for the whole flight
     Throw a bunch of knockout tabs down my throat


—description from the publisher

boardgame: The Naval SITREP: The Journal of Naval Miniatures Wargaming #68
description:
The Naval SITREP is published twice a year by the Admiralty Trilogy Group. It supports the Admiralty Trilogy game system with scenarios, articles on history and technology, and rules expansions & corrections.

The Naval SITREP Issue #68 was published in April of 2025.

Features

Double Breakthrough: The Hai Chi and Hai Chen Mutiny of 1935

CaS Scenario: Battle East of Hong Kong (with supporting annexes)

Battlefield Multitaskers

The Ships and Aircraft of Taiwan (with Annexes A, B, and C-L)

DoB Scenario: Convoy Attack

New NWC book on Chinese Amphibious Capability

Rule Changes and Corrections

Understanding the GBU-31 QUICKSINK

Night Fighting in CaS 5th Edition

Product Updates

Good News About Shapeways

Book Reviews

The Neptune Factor, by Nicholas A Lambert

Fifty-Three Days on Starvation Island, by John R. Bruning

The German Invasion of Norway, April 1940, by Geirr H. Haarr
family: Magazine: The Naval SITREP – Journal of Naval Miniatures Wargaming

description: Squishyland comes with 8 card decks, each geared to a different grade level. Take turns drawing a card, completing the challenge on the card (usually involving phonics), and then moving to the board space designated by the card in Candyland fashion. The first player to reach the final space on the board is the winner.

Assassin's Creed: Animus is a competitive and asymmetric game that spans many popular eras and characters of the iconic franchise, with players seeking to complete their very own adventures in the world of Assassin's Creed.

boardgame: Dominion of the Claymore, Tomahawk and Talwar
description:
This is the Dominion of the Claymore, Tomahawk and Talwar Wargames Rules Solo Game. 
This standalone set of rules covers the Jacobite Rebellion of Bonnie Prince Charlie, the French-Indian War in North America and Canada, the Anglo-Cherokee War, Pontiac's Rebellion, and battles in India and in the West Indies.
